coredata.dc:title: High-efficient, spherical and thermal-stable carbon dots@silica fluorescent composite as rare earth-free phosphors for white LED
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xinguo
Sun, Zishan
Zhu, Zhenpng
Luo, Jiabao
Wu, Zhanchao
Wang, Zhengliang
coredata.dc:description: This paper reported a facile synthesis path to realize high-luminescent rare-earth-free composite powder, by embedding blue/green-emitting N-doped carbon dots into silica nano-spheres (CD@SiO2). Carbon dots were evenly dispersed in CD@SiO2 composites and eventually the aggregation-induced luminescence quenching is eliminated. Compared to the naked CDs, CD@SiO2 composites exhibit perfectly spherical morphology with average size of ~200 nm, significantly enhanced emission intensity (>4 times) and better thermal stability (T 50 > 150 °C). As-synthesized CD@SiO2 own strong blue/green emission with high quantum efficiency (QE) over 60%. White LEDs prototypes with promising properties, i.e. CRI = 89.1, CCT = 4850 K and CIE coordinates = (0.3514, 0.3715), were fabricated by combining blue/green CD@SiO2 and red CdTe@CaCO3 composites with NUV chip. All results indicate that these rare-earth-free, mono-dispersed spherical, and thermal-stable composite materials with efficient blue/green emission could be an exceptional choice as phosphors for LED application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Composite phosphors
Carbon dots
Silica nano-sphere
Luminescence
Light-emitting diodes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns (a) and FT-IR spectra (b) of BCD@SiO2 and GCD@SiO2. Inset is the XRD pattern of CDs.
Representative SEM images (a, d; scale bars are 1.0 μm for each graph), TEM images (b, e; scale bars are 100 nm for each graph), and elemental mapping images (c, f) of BCD@SiO2 and GCD@SiO2

PLE and PL spectra of BCD@SiO2 (a), GCD@SiO2 (b) and the emission color coordinates of two composites in CIE 1931 diagram (c). Insets are corresponding photographs of three samples taken under 365 UV light. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
PL decay curves for BCD@SiO2 and GCD@SiO2 (a) and temperature-dependent PL spectra of BCD@SiO2 (b, λ ex = 360 nm), GCD@SiO2 (c, λ ex = 405 nm).

Electroluminescence spectra of single-chromatic and white LED prototypes based on BCD@SiO2, GCD@SiO2 and RQD@CaCO3 (a–d) with the corresponding CIE coordinates (e). Insets are photographs of the working LED devices at 20 mA current.

head.ce:title: High-efficient, spherical and thermal-stable carbon dots@silica fluorescent composite as rare earth-free phosphors for white LED

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Comprehensive Utilization of Mineral Resource in Ethnic Regions

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Comprehensive Utilization of Mineral Resource in Ethnic Regions, Joint Research Centre for International Cross-border Ethnic Regions Biomass Clean Utilization in Yunnan, School of Chemistry & Environment, Yunnan Minzu University, Kunming, Yunnan, 650500, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper reported a facile synthesis path to realize high-luminescent rare-earth-free composite powder, by embedding blue/green-emitting N-doped carbon dots into silica nano-spheres (CD@SiO2). Carbon dots were evenly dispersed in CD@SiO2 composites and eventually the aggregation-induced luminescence quenching is eliminated. Compared to the naked CDs, CD@SiO2 composites exhibit perfectly spherical morphology with average size of ~200 nm, significantly enhanced emission intensity (>4 times) and better thermal stability (T 50 > 150 °C). As-synthesized CD@SiO2 own strong blue/green emission with high quantum efficiency (QE) over 60%. White LEDs prototypes with promising properties, i.e. CRI = 89.1, CCT = 4850 K and CIE coordinates = (0.3514, 0.3715), were fabricated by combining blue/green CD@SiO2 and red CdTe@CaCO3 composites with NUV chip. All results indicate that these rare-earth-free, mono-dispersed spherical, and thermal-stable composite materials with efficient blue/green emission could be an exceptional choice as phosphors for LED application.

ce:section.ce:para: Presently, phosphors activated by rare-earth dopant (YAG: Ce3+, CaAlN3: Eu2+and BAM: Eu2+) which could efficiently convert UV/blue emission from InGaN chips to green/yellow/red light, is of important for realizing white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) [1–7]. The quantities of rare-earth raw materials are quite scarce and hence they are very expensive. Some of rare-earth chlorides and oxides contain heavy metals, which are toxic and hazardous to both human health and the environment. Besides, the synthesis of rare-earth doped phosphors usually requires sintering process with high temperature and/or pressurized atmosphere [8,9], which is complicated and energy-consuming. Hence, the need for developing novel rare-earth free phosphors for LED application has attracted great attention. the phosphor should be non-toxic, thermally and chemically stable, along with broad absorption band and high-efficient emission. Meanwhile, the fabrication, use, and disposal of the phosphor should be as environmentally benign as possible [10].

Carbon dots (CDs) is known as a new kind of bio-compatible luminescent nanoparticles with merits like good chemical stability, no photobleaching and tunable luminescence with high efficiency [11–14]. However, the excellent luminescence of CDs could only maintain in their colloidal solutions, which would be greatly suppressed by aggregation-induced luminescence quenching effect when CDs are of solid-state [15,16]. This PL quenching is generally caused by the non-radiative decay process with shorten intra-particle distance, which is favorable for the occurrence of energy transfer. Therefore, it is of great important to figure out a facile approach to suppress the occurrence of CDs aggregation in solid state, while maintain CDs high-efficient luminescence in CDs-based composites. To obtain solid-state luminescent materials, CDs should be dispersed into matrix to avoid the aggregation-induced luminescence quenching of CDs. Since there are lots of hydroxyl groups in CDs and SiO2, CDs have a strong interaction with SiO2 through hydrogen bonding, resulting in the mono-disperse CDs in SiO2. Eventually, aggregation-induced luminescence quenching of CDs could be suppressed and efficient emission of CDs could be maintained in solid state.

Herein, we developed a simple method to prepare fluorescent hybrid composites as phosphor for LED application. By embedding blue/green-emitting N-doped CDs into spherical SiO2 matrix (CD@SiO2) using CTAB as template, the aggregation-induced luminescence quenching was suppressed by evenly dispersed CDs in SiO2, then the composite phosphors with bright emission were prepared. The fluorescent properties of as-prepared hybrid composites were systematically explored. Highly luminescent and stable CDs-based composite phosphors are used to fabricate bright single-chromatic (blue/green/red) and white LEDs. The results indicates that these CDs@SiO2 composites are of great potential as rare-earth-free phosphors for LED application.

0.42 g citric acid and 536 μL ethylenediamine were dissolved in DI-water (10 mL) under 5 min ultrasonic exposure. Then the solution was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (40 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After the reaction, the reactors were naturally cooled to room temperature. The product, which was brown-black and transparent, was purified by dialyzing with a cellulose ester membrane bag (Mw = 1000) in order to obtained the corresponding BCD solution.

3 g citric acid and 6 g urea were added into 20 mL deionized water to form transparent solution. Then the mixture was deeply heated about 5 min in a domestic 650 W microwave oven, during which the mixture varied in color and state that from a colorless liquid to a brown and finally dark-brown solid, indicating the formation of GCD. After that, GCD were re-dissolved in ethanol, and purified in a centrifuge to remove the aggregated particles at the speed of 8000 rpm for 5 min. Finally, the yellow-brown GCD solution was obtained.

The CD@SiO2 synthesis was referred from that in previous report [15]. Briefly, 7 mL of ethanol, 0.2 g of CTAB, and 50 μL of diethanolamine were dissolved in 25 mL of water under stirring at 60 °C for 30 min to prepare a transparent solution. Then, 2 mL of tetraethoxysilane was added rapidly. The reaction was finished after stirring for another 2 h. Composite samples were collected by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 10 min) and dried at 60 °C for 3 h. In case of BCD@ SiO2, 25 mL of BCD aqueous solution was used instead of 25 mL of water; For GCD@SiO2 synthesis, 7 mL of GCD ethanol solution was used instead of 7 mL of ethanol.

Prototype single-chromatic and white LED devices were fabricated by integrating the blue, green and red composite phosphors on an InGaN LED chip (λ max = 390 nm). The devices were then encapsulated in a phosphor/silicone resin mixture, with the mixture placed directly on the headers and then cured at 150 °C for 1 h. After packaging was completed, the LED device with phosphor was measured in an integrating sphere under direct current forward-bias condition.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a Rigaku D/max-II B X-ray diffractometerwith graphite-monochromatic Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.1518 nm). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded over the range of 500–4000 cm−1 on an Bruker VERTEX 70 spectrometer with a resolution of 4 cm−1. For IR analysis, the dry powder of composites was dispersed in KBr (2% wt/wt) and pressed as a 13 mm disc with minimum pressure. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM1400, JEOL) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM7001F, JEOL) were utilized to investigate the morphology of all composites. For the TEM investigation, the product was dispersed in ethanol by ultrasonication. The dispersion was dropped onto a carbon-coated copper grid (300-mesh), and the copper grid was then dried at 100 °C for 5 min before the TEM characterization. For elemental analysis, a Carl Zeiss SUPRA 55VP FE-SEM equipped with an EDS detector was used under an applied voltage of 3.0 kV. Room temperature photoluminescent excitation (PLE) and emission (PL) spectra were monitored by an Edinburgh Instruments FLS920 spectrofluorimeter using a continuous 450W xenon lamp as excitation source and a R928 photomultiplier tube as detector. Temperature-dependent PL spectrawere recorded using the same spectrophotometer and detectors. The decay time curves were collected using a 100 W pulsed hydrogen lamp (nF900) on the same spectrophotometer and detectors. The internal quantum efficiency (IQE) was measured using the integrating sphere on an Edinburgh Instruments FLS920 spectrofluorimeter, and a Xe900 lamp was used as an excitation source. The optical properties of the as-fabricated LED Prototypes were obtained using an integrating sphere (Everfine Photo-E-Info Co., Ltd). The IQE values are obtained using an integrated sphere, where the emission area of sample (L S), excitation area of BaSO4 standard (E R) and that of sample (E S) is measured. And IQE = L S/(E R - E S). Besides, the temperature-dependent PL spectra are obtained after the temperature reaches at the setting value and is stabilized for 3 min.

To inspect the composition and functional groups of hybrid composites, XRD and FR-IR analysis was performed. Fig. 1 a shows the XRD patterns of blue- and green-emitting CD@SiO2 samples, in which a broad diffraction peak at 23° is observed and confirms the formation of the amorphous silica. The XRD diffraction (Fig. 1a inset) pattern of CD exhibits a broad peak at 2θ = 23.34°, corresponding to the (002) interlayer spacing of graphitic structure. Fig. 1b shows FTIR spectra of BCD@SiO2 and GCD@SiO2. As marked in Fig. 1b, the vibration peaks at 1070 cm−1 and 793 cm−1 is assigned to ν(Si–O–Si), while those at 1515-1800 cm−1 are due to CH2 and N–H functional groups on CDs surface. The vibration of ν(Si–OH) and ν(O–H) in silica surface corresponds to the peak at ~3440 cm−1 [17,18].

The morphology and composition of as-synthesized composite samples were checked by SEM, TEM and EDS. The highly mono-dispersed BCD@SiO2 (Fig. 2 a) and GCD@SiO2 (Fig. 2d) particles possess a perfectly spherical morphology with smooth surface and a diameter of ~200 and ~250 nm, respectively. A further and clearer observation came from corresponding TEM image (Fig. 2b and e), where well-defined highly mono-dispersed nano-spheres of ~250 nm in diameter are clearly presented, and the diameter of GCD@SiO2 is slightly larger than that of BCD@SiO2. Element mapping by EDS clearly shows the elements of Si, O, C and N evenly distributed in the CD@SiO2 composites (Fig. 2c and f). The spherical shape and smooth surface of obtained composite particles is important to minimize the scattering and refracting loss of both exciting and emitting light, which eventually result in PL enhancement. Meanwhile, the formation of silica spheres embedding CDs could effectively passive the surface of CDs, which could reduce the number of surface trap/defect on CDs as quenching centers and enhance the emission thermal stability.

As seen in Fig.3 a, a broadband emission with peak at 440 nm and full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) of 40 nm is found in the PL spectrum of BCD@SiO2 composite. It is found that both the emission wavelength of BCD@SiO2 were independent on excitation-wavelength, which could be assigned to the uniform size and the surface state of CDs dispersed in composite, and similar result could be found in CDs@POSS solid-state luminophores reported by Rogach et al. [19]. In case of GCD@SiO2 composite, it presents clearly faint yellow color under sunlight, while it emits strong green luminescence under UV light irradiation. Fig. 3b shows the PL and PLE spectra of GCD@SiO2, it can be seen that GCD@SiO2 powder exhibits a green peak at 520 nm with the FWHM of 53 nm under UV excitation. The excitation spectra of both BCD@SiO2 and GCD@SiO2 shows two absorption bands, i.e. 240 and 360 nm for BCD, while 240 and 405 nm for GCD, respectively. The domain absorption peak at 360 or 405 nm is assigned to the trapping of excited state energy from CDs surface states, whose energy is different for CD with different compositions. And the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains is responsible for the minor 240 nm peak [20]. The strong absorption in the near-UV range indicates that both composites could be effectively excited by UV light from InGaN chip.

After the solidification of CDs in the composite particles, the peak wavelengths were blue-shifted from 453 nm (solution) to 440 nm (solid) for BCD, while from 542 nm (solution) to 520 nm (solid) for GCD. Meanwhile, the corresponding emission intensities were enhanced significantly compared with corresponding CDs solution, i.e. 5.64 times for BCD and 4.47 times for BCD, respectively. The possible reasons for luminescent variation in CDs “colloidal state→solid state” transformation could be (1) the minimization of the surface trap states of CDs by forming an appropriate broad band-gap silica shell on CDs [20], (2) the surface passivation of CDs after chemical reactions between functional groups on CDs surface and hydroxyl on silica shell, (3) the shrinking of the CDs' radiative transition gap during the ripening of the silica shell, and (4) the variation of the media's refraction index (i.e. water (1.33), silica (1.45)) [21]. The (1) and (2) corresponds to the PL enhancement, while (3) and (4) are responsible for the blue-shift of emission wavelength.

To achieve a high-color-rendering white light requires efficient blue/green/red-emitting material whose combination could cover a wide color gamut in CIE diagram. The CIE chromaticity coordinates were calculated from their corresponding PL spectrum and depicted in Fig. 3c. The CIE coordinates for BCD@SiO2 and GCD@SiO2 are (0.164, 0.128) and (0.293, 0.503), respectively. These CIE values are quite close to NTSC standard blue (0.155, 0.070) and green (0.310, 0.595). The corresponding photos of the composite samples under 365 nm irradition are shown in Fig. 3c inset. In the true color photographs of BCD@SiO2 and GCD@SiO2 powders under a 365 nm UV lamp, a pure color and strong photoluminescence can be observed.

As reported by Setlur [22] and Su [23], many traditional phosphors based on Eu3+/Tb3+/Mn4+ are not suitable for LEDs application, since those transitions are forbidden with long decay lifetime (>1 ms), which could cause severe quenching due to saturation from the high LED radiation flux on the phosphor. Therefore, phosphor with a fast decay lifetime that prevents saturation quenching is necessary to develop new phosphors specifically for LEDs. The time-resolved photoluminescence decay spectra for CD@SiO2 composites are shown in Fig. 4 a. It is found that the lifetime data of CD@SiO2 composites’ emission could be well fitted by a double-exponential function:
I
( t )
=
I 0
+
A 1
exp
(
− t
/
τ 1
)
+
A 2
exp
(
− t
/
τ 2
)

In which τ 1 and τ 2 are the lifetime components, A 1 and A 2 are the weighting parameters. The measured lifetimes are fitted with bi-exponential decay and similar with the results of previous reported CD-based composite phosphors [24]. Meanwhile, the exact value of each decay components replies on the composition and structure of the material. The average lifetime (τ*) could be calculated by lifetime components and corresponding weighting parameters as:
τ ∗
=
A 1
τ 1 2
+
A 2
τ 2 2
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2

Thus, the average lifetime for BCD@SiO2 and GCD@SiO2 is 15.75 and 10.04 ns, respectively. For comparison, the τ* value of corresponding blue CDs and green CDs in solution state is 14.24 and 6.28 ns, respectively. The results in Fig. 4a indicate that using CDs with emission transitions that have decay times of <100 ns could prevent the above-mentioned saturation-based quenching and result in high-efficient emission. It is found that the CD@SiO2 composite powder have a longer decay lifetime than that of CDs solution. After embedded in SiO2 matrix, the number of surface defects acted as quenching centers was reduced and non-radiative loss by solvent molecule vibration/collision was prohibited due to successful surface passviation. The recombination rate of photoelectrons that populate the excited state will reduce, thereby prolong its luminescence lifetime and enhance its emission intensity.

It is important that the luminescence of converted phosphors in LED application should have high quantum efficiencies to ensure the high luminous output of the LED package [25]. The internal quantum efficiency (IQE) of BCD@SiO2 and GCD@SiO2 samples were measured by an integrating sphere. The GCD@SiO2 exhibits the highest IQE value of 89.52%, followed with BCD@SiO2 with IQE of 64.70%, which is comparable to most of rare-earth doped phosphors and confirms that these composite materials have large potential for application.

The embedding of a suitable inorganic shell could effectively passivate the surface of CDs by reduce the number of the defects and dangling bonds on surface, which are considered as non-radiative recombination centers [26]. In order to evaluate the effect of coating on radiative process, the value of radiative and non-radiative transition rate ((K R, K NR) were calculated using data of lifetime and QE:
τ
a v g
=
1
K R
+
K
N R
; Q E =
K R
K R
+
K
N R
which can be further deduced as:
τ
a v g
=
Q E
K R
; Q E =
1
1 +
K
N R
K R

The K R and K NR values are 4.108 × 107 s−1 and 2.241 × 107 s−1 for BCD@SiO2, 8.916 × 107 s−1 and 1.043 × 107 s−1 for GCD@SiO2 respectively. As comparison, corresponding K R values for BCD and GCD in solution state are 0.808 × 107 s−1 and 3.188 × 107 s−1, respectively. This indicates that SiO2 coated CDs composite materials own high QE due to higher excitonic radiative transition rates than those of naked CDs with low QE.

The emission thermal stability of LED phosphors is vital for LED performance, since the phosphor operating temperature is > 100 °C and the converted phosphors in LED device should have a thermal-stable emission to aviod color deviation and luminous quenching of light output [27]. The temperature-dependent PL spectra of BCD@SiO2 under excitation at 360 nm were measured, and the results are plotted in Fig. 4b. Th spectral shape and intensity of BCD@SiO2 remains almost unchanged with increasing temperature (T) when T < 100 °C, whereas T > 100 °C, its PL intensity exhibits slight and gradual decrease with rising temperature. The integrated emission intensity with rising temperature was displayed in Fig. 4b inset, where the emission intensity of sample at 200 °C remains 66% of room temperature intensity. That means the thermal quenching temperature T 0.5 is higher than 200 °C for BCD@SiO2 phosphor.

The GCD@SiO2 shows a almost zero-thermal-quenching emission up to 130 °C. Fig. 4c presents the temperature-dependent PL spectra of GCD@SiO2 along with the normalized variation of emission intensity in the temperature range 20–200 °C, which remains 88% of RT intensity at 200 °C. The reported thermally stable green phosphors, K2Ba5Si12O30: Eu2+ [28], (Ba,Ca)3Si6O12N2: Eu2+ [29] and NaBaScSi2O7: Eu2+ [30] is of 60%, 70% and 75% of RT intensity at 200 °C, respectively. The exact reasons for emission thermal quenching in CDs remains unknown, while previous studies suggested that it might be related to thermal-induced population of non-radiative channels of surface (trap/defect) states [31]. In CD@SiO2 composite, the interactions such as bonding or hybrid condensation between functional groups on CDs surface and hydroxyl on silica shell would passive the surface of CDs, which could greatly reduce the number of quenching centers such as surface trap/defect on CDs. Such a surface modification consequently results in enhanced emission temperature stability of our as-obtained CD@SiO2 composites.

To investigate the potential of the as-synthesized materials as LED phosphors, a series of single-chromatic LED prototypes were fabricated by pre-coating CD@SiO2 and our previous-reported QD@CaCO3 [26] composite materials onto a 390 nm-emitting InGaN chip. Corresponding electroluminescence (EL) spectra of single-chromatic LED devices under 20 mA current are shown in Fig. 5 a–c. Bright blue, green and red light could be observed in the photos of operated LED devices (Fig. 5a–c inset), which indicates that CD@SiO2 and QD@CaCO3 composite material exhibits efficient single-chromatic emission after absorbing NUV light from LED chip. Fig. 5d shows the EL spectrum of the white LED prototype, in which one sharp UV peak at 390 nm from LED chip, and three broad visible bands at 440, 520 and 660 nm from composite phosphors could be observed. The CIE coordinates of white emission is (0.3514, 0.3715) and close to standard white (0.333, 0.333). Meanwhile, this white emission possess high color-rendering index (CRI = 89.1) and low color temperature (CCT = 4850 K), which is better than YAG: Ce3+-based white LED (CCT = 6500 K, CRI = 75) [27] and CDs-based white LED (CCT = 4850 K, CRI = 70.5) [32]. The high color quality of four LED prototypes is illustrated in the CIE1931 chromaticity diagram in Fig. 5e, along with the corresponding CIE coordinates.

A facile way to achieve high-efficient, thermal-stable and rare-earth-free composite phosphors were realized by embedding blue/green-emitting CDs into silica nano-sphere, respectively. The resulting CD@SiO2 composite luminophore not only possesses efficient blue/green emission with high PL QYs exceeding 60%, but also high thermal stability (T 50 > 200 °C). By combination of these high-luminescent composites with NUV LED chip, single-chromatic and white LED prototypes were fabricated. Besides, white emission was realized by combining tri-color composite materials with UV InGaN chip. This white emission possess high color-rendering index (CRI = 89.1), low color temperature (CCT = 4850 K) and suitable CIE color coordinates of (0.3514, 0.3715). All results indicate that these rare-earth-free, mono-dispersed spherical, and thermal-stable composite materials with efficient blue/green emission could be an exceptional choice as phosphors for LED application.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled, “High-efficient, spherical and thermal-stable carbon dots@silica fluorescent composite as rare earth-free phosphors for white LED”.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21601081 and 21661033), Guangzhou Scientific planning program (No. 201804010260), Open project of College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology (QUSTHX201925) and the Applied Basic Research Project of Yunnan Province (2017FB017). We thank Dr. Peichan Chen (College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xiamen University, China) for elemental and TEM analysis, and Mr. Chengyi Xu (College of Chemistry, Jilin University, China) for QE measurement and Prof. Zhengliang Wang (School of Chemistry & Environment, Yunnan Minzu University, China) for LED fabrication and characterization. The corresponding author (X.G. Zhang) sincerely thanks his beloved, Miss. Lucia (Yingcong) Chen (Guangzhou Science Technology and Innovation Commission), for her help in polishing the article.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Progress in discovery and structural design of color conversion phosphors for LEDs

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Xia, Q. Liu, Progress in discovery and structural design of color conversion phosphors for LEDs, Prog. Mater. Sci. 2016, 84, 59-117.

Lian, Composition screening in blue-emitting Li4Sr1+xCa0.97–x(SiO4)2: Ce3+ phosphors for high quantum efficiency and thermally stable photoluminescence, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces. DOI: 10.1021/acsami.7b08671.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced photoluminescence emission and thermal stability from introduced cation disorder in phosphors

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mn4+ doped fluorotitanate phosphors: synthesis, optical properties and application in LED devices
